data.title: 8 High Yields Of Quality And Value To Buy
data.description:
Rose’s Income Garden “RIG” portfolio contains 75 dividend-paying investments in 11 sectors, where 47+ have investment-grade S&P credit ratings. The remainder of those not rated are financials, preferred shares, or ETFs. The portfolio has a forward yield of 6.09%, with most of the financials and ETFs offering the highest yields, but again, they are mostly rarely rated. This article is about some of the highly S&P-rated ones with nice higher yields that might just interest you to purchase.

8 Quality S&P rated companies with 4.7% - 10% Yield

The first 2 charts are a review for:

1- Dividend % Yield

2- Price to Earnings or Funds from Operations

Lastly comes the company information, charts, and Rose's recommendation.

The companies in the first chart below are presented from the highest to lowest yield.

1- Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC) - 9.95% yield

This business development company/BDC specializes in growth capital primarily in the USA for middle-market companies. It is headquartered in LA, CA, and was founded in 2004. It has a BBB S&P credit rating, which is somewhat rare for a BDC to be rated at all. It has maintained a $1.92 dividend since 2023 and on occasion will offer special dividends but has not done so recently. It has announced the same quarterly dividend of 48c with an ex-date of March 13th and a pay date of the 30th.

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

The “SA” chart indicates a forward P/E of ~10x, making it close to a buy by that metric. It has a price/book of 0.98% from M* as of today and therefore is just below fair value.

Rose's recommendation

At a 10% yield or more is when I get interested in buying more.

It is a good value at a Goldilocks price right now along with having a quality S&P credit rating.

2- Altria Group, Inc. (MO) - 6.33% yield

This consumer staple worldwide company that offers oral and smokeable tobacco and nicotine products was founded in 1822 and is headquartered in Richmond, VA. It has a 5-year DGR of 4.12% and an S&P credit rating of BBB+. It pays quarterly and just raised the dividend by 4c to $1.06 or 2.9%, goes ex-dividend next in March for payment in April. It has paid rising dividends now for 56 years.

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

The P/E is over normal along with the M* fair value. Earnings estimates from FG suggest 3-4% up over the next few years. Not very exciting, and that could account for the lower 2.9% pay raise.

Rose's recommendation

It is quality but has risen in price quickly, and I would wait for a dip to add more, but the over 6% yield is tempting if you don’t own it.

3- VICI Properties Inc. (VICI) - 6.2% yield

VICI Properties Inc. is considered a casino real estate REIT, is headquartered in NY, NY, and has a BBB- S&P credit rating.

From Yahoo Finance in Dec 2025:

“It owns one of the largest portfolios of market-leading gaming, hospitality, wellness, entertainment, and leisure destinations, including Caesars Palace Las Vegas, MGM Grand, and the Venetian Resort Las Vegas, three of the most iconic entertainment facilities on the Las Vegas Strip. VICI Properties owns 93 experiential assets across a geographically diverse portfolio consisting of 54 gaming properties and 39 other experiential properties across the United States and Canada.”

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

The statistics I show are for P/AFFO, which is from “FG” and differs from P/FFO shown above, but the bottom line is that it is currently undervalued. Earnings are looking to rise by ~3% this year and 2027 using FG estimates. It has a 5 year DGR of 7.08%, and it just raised it to 45c in September 2025 from 43.3%, or 3.9%, so the raise amount is on the downtrend.

Rose's recommendation

I like it under $30, better near $28. Note that M* has a higher price target, making it still a buy, and if a 6.1% yield is attractive for your portfolio, then it's time to buy some. I am waiting for another dip to add on in RIG.

4 - Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) - 5.68% yield

This worldwide communications company, formerly known as Bell Atlantic, incorporated in 1983 and changed its name in 2000. It provides technology, information, and entertainment products and is headquartered in NY, NY. It has a 5-year DGR of ~2% and a record of raising the dividend for 21 years.

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

It reliably pays quarterly, announcing a raise in October from 67.75c to 69c, or 1.8%, which was just paid most recently on February 2nd. It just announced an early raise to 70.75c, or 2.5%, to go ex April 10th and be paid May 1st. Surprise, surprise, and that caused its price to pop as well. M* has it undervalued with a 4* rating, suggesting it is still a buy with an FV of $53.

Rose's recommendation

This quality, reliable income stock has now gotten just a bit pricey, with the yield down to 5.7%. My PT is $47.25 even with the new yearly raise to $2.83 yearly. That price gives it a 6% yield, which is the minimum I want it to be, just for me.

5- British American Tobacco (BTI) - 5.18% yield

This consumer staple company that offers tobacco and nicotine products worldwide was founded in 1902 and is headquartered in London, UK. It has a 5-year DGR of 1.86% and a BBB+ S&P credit rating. It pays quarterly, with the latest being 81.3c, which was just paid on February 9th. It did just announce a full year of dividends with a raise to 61.26p, or 84c if I converted it correctly. I got the information from the following article in “SA” Feb. 12th. “

The actual amount received will vary according to the foreign exchange rate.

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

The normal P/E is a lot lower than it is now, as this stock was terribly undervalued for multiple years and is finally catching up to what might actually be considered more normal. Earnings are estimated to rise by 7-8% for the next 2 years, and it could be that the price is catching up with those expectations.

Rose's recommendation

It was purchased for RIG a long time ago at quite a nice lower price and quite a higher yield. I personally believe it is now overvalued but remain pleased with the new dividend announcement and hold it.

6- NNN REIT (NNN) - 5.5% yield

This is a retail triple net REIT that operates in 48 states and has headquarters in Orlando, FL, with a BBB+ S&P credit rating. It has a 5 year DGR of 2.66% and a record of raising dividends for 36 years. It pays quarterly 60c and went ex on Jan. 30th, which will be paid on Feb. 13th.

The current P/AFFO is 12.47x, and the norm is higher at 13.84x, which indicates it is somewhat undervalued.

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

Rose's recommendation

Quality, reliable dividend payer with a nice, fair, rather decent yield. It could still have some capital gains, as M* gives it a price target of $49.21. I believe it is fair value and would want more cheaper for my RIG portfolio, and a 6% yield would be down near a $40 price, with my fingers crossed for that to happen.

7- W. P. Carey Inc. (WPC) - 5.12% yield

WP Carey is a large international industrial triple net real estate REIT headquartered in NY., NY. It has a BBB+ S&P credit rating. It operates offices in NY, London, Amsterdam, and Dallas, TX.

Quarterly payer WP Carey usually raises its dividend each quarter, not yearly as most others do. The important takeaway is that the price has been rising, and one could say catching up to where it had been once upon a time. The yield is holding steady above 5% with the most recent raises. I and many other investors lost confidence in management when it split off the office portion of its portfolio without adequate warning and notice of a dividend cut coming as well.

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

The current P/AFFO is 14.3x, and the normal is 13.80x, which makes it just a bit overvalued using that metric. Earnings are suggested to grow 3-4% over the next 2 years using FG.

Rose's recommendation

I believe this quality company is now overvalued, with the yield trending down faster than desired. I will look to perhaps trim it if the price rises up to near $75 -80.

8- Kimberly-Clark Corp. (KMB) - 4.73% yield

Kimberly-Clark Corp., in the consumer staple sector, provides household and personal care products, is headquartered in Dallas, TX, and was founded in 1872. It has an "A" S&P credit rating while being a dividend champion/aristocrat that has paid rising dividends for 54 years.

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

The normal P/E is 19.68 and it currently sells at 13.84x, being undervalued using that metric.

Earnings are expected to rise slowly at 1-2% over the next 2 years, which could be the reason for some of that lower price. The January dividend of $1.26 was paid on the 5th. It just announced the next quarterly dividend and a raise of 2c to $1.28. It is disappointing but does go along with the lower estimated earnings.

Rose's recommendation

For a non-tobacco consumer staple, this is still quite a high yield with 3 dividends left for 2026. This is just a nice quality company with a reliable dividend and hopefully some capital gains coming in the future to match the M* fair value of $133. In full disclosure, I did just add at the 5% yield and would do so again if there is a dip.

Summary/Conclusion

RIG, with its 75 investments, is hitting all-time highs for portfolio value and is up 6.7% YTD. For today, while maintaining a 6.05% forward yield. It is also beating SPY by 6.14% since its inception in November 2021.

data.title: Operational Progress, Macro Headwinds Drive Resideo Technologies
data.description:
Stronger Platform, Softer End Markets

Resideo Technologies (REZI), which provides comfort, energy management, and safety and security solutions, displays an interesting journey. It has scaled its B2B platform by leveraging customer stickiness. It taps into the Connected Home growth engine by introducing a range of high-value, differentiated products in recent times. It has streamlined its operations with the exit of the non-core Grid Services and the elimination of the legacy Honeywell liabilities.

But the focus on margins also highlights the lack of a compelling underlying investment option. The HVAC market is going through a disruption (transitioning to new low-GWP refrigerants). This brought out inventory issues that are typically cyclical and timing-related. On a positive note, the cash flow problem appears to be temporary. In my view, the management can resolve it with the completion of the indemnification payment. The stock’s current valuation multiples appear reasonable compared to its peers. So, I recommend a “Hold,” unchanged from my previous iteration.

Housing Market And Inventory Challenges

The macro factors may not be in REZI’s favor, but the current trend indicates the wind may return. By late 2025, I see indications that the new residential homebuilding lacks firepower, which will stifle the repair and remodeling market. The U.S. mortgage rate stretched out over the past year. In the past year until October 2025, the privately owned housing units in the US decreased by ~8%, while the US mortgage rate (30-year average) expanded by more than 200 basis points. However, since October, mortgage rates have declined.

The softness in the Residential housing and HVAC (heating, ventilation, and air conditioning) markets disrupted REZI’s demand and inventory. It led to refrigerant-related inventory issues late in Q3. It is generally observed that the U.S. HVAC industry is transitioning to low-GWP refrigerants under new regulations. So, the legacy HVAC equipment stockpile slowed new demand. However, its management sees the HVAC disruption as temporary. It expects normalization following policy clarity over the next few quarters.

M&A and Other Strategies

REZI’s management sounded confident in the Q3 earnings call about achieving profitable growth through differentiated Connected Home products. Investors may note that Resideo primarily operates through two segments - Products & Solutions and ADI Global Distribution. Its expansion in security and residential AV (audio-visual) should bring the desired scale to its operations. In this context, I discussed the benefits of a combined Rezi and Snap One. The deal was struck in June 2024 for $1.4 billion. Subsequently, REZI’s customer engagement and cross-sales opportunities increased through AI adoption.

The evidence of REZI’s focus on core operations is found in its disposal of the Grid Services demand response business in December. The divestiture will enable its home energy management through third-party platform integrations.

In the ADI segment, I expect a robust e-commerce execution to strengthen its competitive positioning. For example, the company’s Omnichannel B2B platform is a fully integrated digital and physical distribution model. Also note that ADI serves more than 100,000 professional customers annually. For this segment, e-commerce can play a significant role in order placement, product discovery, and fulfillment efficiency.

Q4 and FY2025 Outlook

According to REZI’s forecast, its revenues can increase marginally (at the guidance mid-point) in Q4 2025 compared to Q3. Its adjusted EBITDA is expected to increase by 2%. It can also generate an EPS in the range of $0.42–$0.52.

In FY2025, REZI expects to record $7.45 billion in revenue (at the guidance mid-point), a 10% rise compared to FY2024. It raised cash from operations guidance to $410–$450 million. The company will likely record an adjusted EBITDA of $818M–$832M.

My Estimates

Over the past 20 quarters, REZI’s adjusted EBITDA increased by 3% on average. I do not expect growth to rebound sharply in the short term because the macroeconomic factors are not bullish, and the headwinds from HVAC inventory disruptions can adversely affect margin. However, new product categories and the impact of the acquisitions will slowly translate into improved revenue stream and higher margins. Over the next four quarters, I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase by 2%-4%.

Risks and Catalysts

So, I think two factors can heavily influence REZI’s Q1 2026 numbers – mortgage rates and HVAC inventory movement. If the mortgage rate reverses, as recent indicators show (the average 30-year mortgage rate declined by 6 basis points), it will support housing turnover and remodeling activity. This can lead to a higher demand for the company’s services/products and improve its margin. On the other hand, if the HVAC inventory continues to accumulate before it eases, sales growth will be deferred.

My estimate is that the REZI’s revenue and EBITDA numbers in Q1 will track the Q4’05 figures. Its quarter-over-quarter revenue growth was 2%, on average, for the past 20 quarters. It can improve to ~3% in Q1. Adjusted EBITDA can stick to the 3% average in Q1.

Analyzing Q3 Segment Results

As disclosed in the earnings report released on November 5, the company's operating segments posted a strong margin growth despite a modest revenue rise. Robust demand for new products like First Alert connected smoke, ElitePRO smart thermostat, and CO (carbon monoxide) detectors drove its performance in Q3. The improvement was distributed across its sales channels, including Retail, OEM, and electrical distribution channels. Both volume and prices improved in Q3.

Ordinarily, higher operating income translates to an increased cash flow from operations. But the chain broke for REZI. In fact, cash flow from operations turned significantly negative ($1.4 billion) in 9M 2025 compared to a positive cash flow a year ago.

The dip was tied to a non-recurring cash outflow from the Honeywell indemnification termination. In August, REZI paid $1.59 billion in cash to Honeywell to terminate a long-standing Indemnification Agreement. The termination of the agreement marks REZI’s elimination of contingent risk, simplification of its structure, and gaining full financial independence from Honeywell. Because the indemnification payment was partly funded with borrowing, the company’s debt/equity (leverage) ratio deteriorated as of September 30, 2025. Investors may note that Resideo was spun off from Honeywell in 2018.

Relative Valuation And Target Price

REZI appears to be reasonably valued compared to its peers. Why? Because the rise in the forward EV/EBITDA multiple (versus the TTM ratio) suggests a premium over its competitors. Its TTM EV/EBITDA ratio (10.7x) falls short of the competitors’ CSW Industrials (CSW), Griffon Corporation (GFF), and Hayward Holdings (HAYW) average. However, the current multiple is higher than its five-year average multiple of 9.7x. So, I think, its relative valuation is within an acceptable range.

As I discussed earlier in the article, I expect a modest 2%—5% adjusted EBITDA growth in the next four quarters. Feeding these values into the EV calculation and assuming the forward EV/EBITDA multiple holds, the stock can trade between $39 and $41, implying a ~10% upside in the near term. In calculating the expected EV, I have also adjusted excess liquidity, defined as total liquidity less three months’ operating costs. I estimate that it has an excess liquidity of $443 million.

I think the stock’s 32% jump over the past six months has put off the returns potential despite the strong long-term drivers. Of course, the near-term margin pressure can put off investors’ enthusiasm. So, the sell-side analysts’ 24% expected returns seem overly optimistic.

What’s The Take On REZI?

I think REZI is navigating a soft housing and HVAC market. Therefore, it pursues discipline rather than chasing growth. The ongoing housing weakness and refrigerant-related inventory disruption have weakened the near-term demand. Cash flows going into the negative territory in 9M 2025 can ring an alarm bell for investors. But the issue relates to a past indemnification termination and has very limited bearing on its current operation. So, it should be resolved going forward.

However, these issues appear temporary. I think the company’s foundation has remained robust. It has kept its focus on Connected Home, security, and AV products. Its ADI segment’s e-commerce adoption helped smooth out execution. The company’s operating margin expansion in Q3 proves that its strategies have been working well. Its balance sheet is overleveraged. Also, the stock appears overvalued compared to its peers. So, I maintain my “Hold” call.

data.title: Skip Uber; Buy Lyft But Only For A Short Ride
data.description:
Why I Write about Uber and Lyft

My followers know that I love to analyze companies that are effectively competing against each other in a duopoly or quasi-duopoly dynamic, whether it is Eli Lilly vs. Novo Nordisk in the weight loss drug market, or BJ's Wholesale vs. Costco in the consumer goods price club space. Such comparisons tend to provide insights into the respective companies' competitive advantages and disadvantages, as well as arbitrage opportunities for investors when their stock valuations diverge to an extent greater than warranted by the differences in their business fundamentals.

With further ado, let's dive into Uber Technologies (UBER) and Lyft (LYFT), each operating a ride-hailing platform. It works as follows: individual riders in need of ride-hailing/taxi services (although both companies call it rideshare) through the mobile app of UBER or LYFT will be matched with a driver, and the driver who wishes to be a chauffeur for a stranger takes up the job. A rider will pay the platform the cost of his ride plus tips to the driver; the platform deducts a substantial portion thereof as its service charges/revenue, leaving the balance to the driver. Each driver is deemed to be an independent contractor, not an employee of Uber or Lyft, as the driver owns and operates their own car at work. In short, Uber and Lyft are the biggest taxi medallion owners in the US (without having to own any cars) that receive commissions for connecting drivers and riders. It is an asset-light platform business.

Overview of the Two Companies

The table immediately below, which I compiled using SA's Peer Comparison function (all other tables and data, unless otherwise indicated, are from SA), provides an overview of the scales of operations and market caps of the two competitors.

Apparently, Mr. Market values UBER stock much higher relative to the disparity between their revenues, workforce sizes, and their market shares (as calculated by Bloomberg above).

An important footnote is due here for the 2.2 P/E GAAP TTM ratio for Lyft. This stems from the fact that LYFT recognizes a "$2.9 billion benefit from the release of our valuation allowance of U.S. federal and certain state deferred tax assets in the fourth quarter of 2025" (Footnote 1 of its 2025 annual income statement here). In plain English, it means that Lyft can use its losses accumulated from many years of operations to offset future taxable incomes, effectively reducing its future tax liabilities by $2.9 billion. In a way, it is a hint that Lyft management now reasonably believes that the company may turn profitable in the foreseeable future.

As measured by price/sales, EV/sales, or price/cash flow, UBER is valued at twice or thrice that of LYFT.

There are usually a few factors that could contribute to such a valuation premium attached to Uber's stock price: maybe Uber is much more efficiently operated and therefore more profitable (after all, ridesharing is a commodity service), or Uber grows much faster, or Uber enjoys more favorable market sentiment, or a combination of the above factors.

Let's start with the top lines. It appears that UBER has grown its revenues at 36% or so, or about 1.5 times as fast as LYFT during the last five years. However, both companies have slowed down considerably during the last three years, with both growing at mid-teens. I suppose that is because the rideshare market has somewhat matured. Looking forward, UBER does not seem to grow substantially faster than LYFT.

There are no meaningful EBIT, EBITDA or net income, or EPS for either company during the last three years, which probably means that neither of them has had positive EBIT, EBITDA net income, or EPS for all of the last three years.

The same note regarding LYFT tax-related accounting earnings from the year 2025 applies here, which explains the extraordinary GAAP EPS growth YoY of Lyft.

In terms of levered FCF, UBER grows about twice as fast as Lyft during the last three years, which may help explain part of the valuation differential here.

While it is difficult to be precise here given that both companies are not truly mature and stable with steady growth rates yet, it appears that their growth rate differentials cannot fully explain the valuation premium assigned to UBER stock relative to the modest valuation of LYFT stock.

It appears that they have comparable gross margins. As disclosed in their respective Form 10-K, for both companies, revenues primarily consist of fees paid by drivers for use of their platforms, usually not the gross receipts riders pay for their rides. Costs of revenues primarily consist of costs directly related to generating said revenue through the platform, such as insurance costs, payment processing charges. The differences between revenues and costs of revenues are their gross profits, and they have comparable gross profit margins.

Their revenues per employee are also comparable. So in terms of workforce productivity at generating top lines, LYFT is actually slightly ahead of UBER. In terms of cash generated from operations, UBER has generated about 8.6 times the cash of LYFT, but remember UBER has over 25 times LYFT's market cap.

Since LYFT has gotten a large one-off accounting profit that has probably somewhat distorted metrics such as return on equity and net income margin, let's focus on other profitability ratios.

Now we see that they started with comparable gross margins, but LYFT has only managed to produce slightly negative EBIT and EBITDA margins, whereas UBER has been able to achieve low teens for both EBIT and EBITDA margins.

This snapshot of their most recent profitability picture is not only messy but only captures the last measuring period. However, it appears that between the lines under gross profits and before EBITDA, LYFT has proportionately more SG&A, R&D or other costs and expenses that have led to its lower EBIT and EBITDA margins.

It is fair to say that UBER has achieved a 10% or so EBIT margin, as compared to LYFT's -2.7% EBIT margin, and this to a large extent explains the valuation premium assigned to UBER stock.

Let's review both companies' 10-year 'profitability history to see if we can confirm this, and spot any long term trends between their profitability profiles.

As indicated in the table below, it takes UBER about five years to reduce its SG&A expenses from about 70% of its revenue to the low 40s in 2021, and about four more years to 15.5% for Y2025. UBER's R&D expenses fluctuated between around 15% and 37% for the first five years and have now come down to around 8% in Y 2025. UBER's other operating expenses ranged between two-thirds and over 100% of its annual revenue for the first five years, but have come down to 23.6% of its Y2025 revenues.

The long-term declines of Uber's SG&A expenses, R&D expenses, and operating expenses as a percentage of revenue apparently have resulted from the economy of scale Uber has achieved. For example, as its revenue grew over 13-fold from $3.8 billion in 2016 to $52.0 billion in 10 years, its SG&A only grew 3.2 times from $2.6 billion to $8.1 billion, and its operating expenses only grew 3.3 times from $3.8 billion to $12.3 billion.

With the economy of scale at its tailwind, only until Y2023 did Uber see its first positive operating income, which was around 3% of its annual revenue. By Y2024, it had risen to 6.4%, and by Y2025, it had risen to 10.7%. It is fair to say that Uber has come a long way from its early days of steep losses to its current positive operating incomes.

As indicated in the table above, LYFT has managed to bring down its SG&A expenses from about 173% of its revenue to the low 40% in 2021 and 2022, and it hovered around 30% during Y2025 and Y2025. It is much higher than UBER's 15% or so in Y2025.

LYFT R&D expenses fluctuated between around the low teens and around 40% for the first five years, but during the last five years, they came down to around 7% in Y2026, actually slightly lower than UBER's 8% in Y2025.

LYFT's other operating expenses ranged between 72% and 192% of its annual revenue for the first five years, but have come down to 37.2% of its Y2025 revenues. It is still significantly higher than Uber's 23.6% of its Y2025 revenues.

The long term declines of Lyft's SG&A expenses, R&D expenses, and operating expenses as a percentage of revenue apparently have also resulted from the economy of scale it has achieved. For example, as its revenue grew over 18.4-fold from $343 million in 2016 to $6.32 billion in 10 years, its SG&A only grew 3.1 times from $594 million to $1.86 billion, and its operating expenses only grew 3.5 times from $659 million to $2.31 billion.

With this economy of scale at its tailwind, Lyft has reduced its operating losses from over 200% of its annual revenue in 2016 to only 3% of its annual revenue in 2025, edging closer to producing its first-ever positive annual operating income.

What is Next?

Based upon the foregoing, the fundamental question for any investor of LYFT has become: can LYFT continue its trend of increasing economies of scale during the last 10 years or so to follow in the footsteps of Uber into achieving profitability, thereby achieving a similar valuation of its stock? If that occurs, LYFT stock price should re-rate. If it catches up with UBER in terms of EBIT margin in the next 2-3 years, its stock price may well double.

There are only a few means of achieving that. First, LYFT can increase its revenue without incurring a proportionate amount of expenses, such as hiring a proportionate number of new employees. The challenges here for that to occur are, first, the ride-hailing market in the US has to leave enough room for LYFT to expand, and its much larger rival, UBER, cannot stop LYFT from grabbing its market share.

Since passenger car transportation service is essentially a commodity, if LYFT is willing and able to engage in a prolonged price war against UBER, it should grow its market share. Of course, that requires a considerable amount of capital and a sustained long-term commitment.

On that note, I disagree with the company's recent decision to use its cash generated from operations to repurchase $1 billion of its stock. In the short term, it may serve the interests of shareholders by setting a floor for its stock price. However, I believe the fund would be better used to aggressively grab market shares through price cuts and marketing campaigns. After all, the stock price will take care of itself if the business grows, and a company should buy back its shares when there is no better use of its capital.

Second, LYFT can aggressively improve its operational efficiency and cut its costs and expenses. LYFT's revenue per employee is slightly higher than that of UBER already. So LYFT shall evaluate any potential means to cut its other expenses if doing so will not materially adversely affect its operational capacity or quality of its service.

If the answer to this fundamental question is yes, then LYFT at today's valuation shall be a modest BUY.

The fundamental question for any investor of UBER, on the other side of the coin, is whether UBER can defend and retain its current market dominance, thereby preserving its profitability and market cap?

Once again, since both companies are providing a commoditized service, in the long term it is actually rather difficult for the incumbent leader to continue its dominance if the challenger is sufficiently determined and competent. After all, the gaps between them—that is, Uber's >25 times the market cap of LYFT and >8 times LYFT's revenue and employee headcount—are still too large for a duopoly. LYFT has all of the operational infrastructure to provide the same commoditized service to drivers and riders alike, and its management is competent in utilizing such infrastructure, LYFT should be able to narrow the gap with UBER.

On that note, if I have to choose between the shares of UBER and LYFT, especially given their valuation differential today, I will buy LYFT and skip UBER, if I believe that in the intermediate future LYFT will turn profitable thereby benefiting from a re-rating of its share price.

Risks to My Thesis

The biggest risk to my thesis is simple: LYFT stock may be cheap for good reasons. Ridesharing is ultimately a scale game, and UBER may already have reached a size that Lyft cannot realistically match. With a much larger revenue base, UBER can spend more on marketing or subsidies when necessary and still remain profitable. If that structural gap persists, LYFT may continue to be left behind, struggling to generate meaningful margins, in which case today’s low valuation would turn out to be a value trap rather than a bargain.

More broadly, I am not convinced that either company truly has a strong moat. Calling these platforms “technology companies” may sound fashionable, but at the end of the day they are selling rides from point A to point B. Riders usually open both apps and pick whichever one is cheaper or arrives faster. That looks much more like the airline business than a software business. Airlines have existed for decades and yet rarely generate high returns on capital. Both Uber and Lyft could end up fighting constant price wars, as airlines do, with limited long-term profitability

Finally, there is the longer-term risk from autonomous driving. If self-driving fleets such as Waymo become real at scale, the economics could change completely. Instead of asset-light platforms such as LYFT and UBER matching drivers and riders, the revenue of ride-hailing may go to the companies that own the capital and cars. That would weaken the advantages both companies currently enjoy from their network of drivers and riders, or even destroy their business models.

For the foregoing reasons, I rate LYFT as a modest BUY for investors who believe that in the intermediate future it will turn profitable, thereby benefiting from a re-rating of its share price, and I would avoid both stocks as long-term investments.

data.title: Snowflake: From Overhyped To Fairly Valued - But Not Out Of The Woods (Upgrade)
data.description:
My sell call on Snowflake (SNOW) in July 2025 has aged well. The stock is trading ~22% below levels in July, but it has had more to do with a broader reset in software stocks as much as some of my concerns getting validated. In July, my sell position was primarily resting on three pillars. One, valuations were stretched. Two, AI monetization lacked proof. And three, operating leverage looked poor for the stage of maturity.

Since my call, Snowflake actually first rallied by a further ~20%, before turning toward negative territory. With corrections now in place, several of my earlier concerns appear invalid. Snowflake does not appear priced for perfection anymore. The positioning today looks like high-quality growth with emerging AI monetization compared to July's high-expectation AI narrative. This calls for a rating upgrade to a Hold.

Growth and AI Inflection

Let us see where the growth story was in July last year. Growth was in the mid-20s and decelerating for a few quarters earlier. The AI optionality was also priced in but not monetized. NRR had drifted down from ~140% to the low 130s. Any further growth downtrend or delay in AI monetization was a factor, particularly when EV to forward revenue was closer to 16x.

The growth deceleration trend was broken in Q2 FY2026 (above 30%) and has settled near the high twenties in Q3. The product revenue guidance for the full year was raised in Q3 FY2026 and the expected exit growth rate of ~27% in Q4, definitely shows continued stability in growth, if not a rebound (which may look marginal). RPO in Q3 was also up ~37% YoY. The growth trajectory, overall, challenges my maturing stagnation concerns that were valid in July last year.

AI monetization progress has also been visible. Although still in early stages, a $100m AI revenue run rate, a quarter earlier than anticipated, is a strong early indicator. Snowflake described the growth in Snowflake Intelligence as the fastest ramp in company history. AI is also influencing half the bookings, which is where it becomes more realistically positioned to expand the pipeline, not just incrementally help upsells.

The growth resilience and early AI monetization signals (along with the valuation compression we will discuss later) are enough to move from a more valuation-based Sell call to a neutral Hold. But the growth and AI story are not strong enough for Snowflake to be labeled a buy either. I look at Snowflake's AI (Cortex, Container, Arctic) layer as a little more than a defensive feature - not a table stakes development that merely keeps core revenues intact and the offering relevant. Actually, by making unstructured data queryable, Snowflake has moved from what was earlier simply unmonetized data storage to GPU-intensive processing that accelerates overall consumption.

However, there are risks around model access getting commoditized. Hyperscalers are integrating native AI into their platforms, and developer-focused alternatives like Databricks are aggressively positioning themselves as AI-first stacks. If data processing capabilities become more competitive due to native AI alternatives, Snowflake's positioning as just the data storage layer will not be great news for pricing power. This is a central tension that has affected long-duration bets in the SaaS and software stocks in the recent past. And I don't have enough clarity on which way AI will swing for Snowflake today. In some cases, like Intuit and Intapp, I have found the AI threat a lesser concern because of regulatory moats, and have issued Buys despite the recent sell-off. In the case of Snowflake, the balance between threats and opportunities due to AI is more nuanced, making high-conviction buys more difficult despite the correction.

Apart from clarity on the AI economics, I will await further transformative momentum signals from Snowflake's AI layer, even in terms of revenue contribution (currently 2-3%). I will also see how growth holds up over the next couple of quarters and look for signs of another re-acceleration. The current levels do not support a sell, but growth and valuations taken together might still mean a fairly valued position, not necessarily a buy. The NRR has also dropped further from ~130% in July 2025 to ~125% in Q3. Any fall below 120% from here could trigger a cautious view for forward durable growth, with the installed base not expanding fast enough to offset the natural maturation of earlier deployments.

Margins Progress Decent

The operating margin picture has changed materially from the time of my earlier thesis. The GAAP operating margins have moved from around -40% to around -27% today. Non-GAAP operating margins are in double digits (up 4.5 percentage points YoY in Q3). Gross margins are stable, and expense ratios are structurally trending down. Both SG&A and R&D expenses are marginally down as a percentage of revenues.

An Upgrade, But Not A Buy

I see improvements in many areas, such as margins and AI traction. Growth resilience also allays some of the deterioration concerns I had in July last year. That, when combined with a valuation compression from ~16x EV to forward revenues to ~10x, is sufficient to upgrade the thesis from a Sell to a Hold. The risk-reward asymmetry that was evident to me in July last year, is no longer the same, with Snowflake having moved closer to a fair valuation territory, without material deterioration in any of the headline metrics that were concerning earlier.

However, I remain skeptical about sustained long duration growth and structural threats that a commoditization of the data processing layer due to AI could bring. If I were given a ~10x multiple in July, I may have double upgraded this thesis to a Buy, but markets are now more cautious about long-duration growth, and some of the uncertainty around that is valid in my view. Hence, Snowflake is best rated a Hold, acknowledging its execution thus far, but waiting on the sidelines for more clarity to emerge.

There are several important angles to keep an eye on in the upcoming quarterly results (Q4 FY2026). A guidance of growth levels in the high twenties for the next financial year will mean the forward PE valuations will be reset even lower (although marginally)—current expectations are in the mid-twenties. Margin progress will also be watched.

Beyond these, I will focus on three additional indicators. First, NRR trajectory will be critical. Stability around ~125% would signal that AI and data workloads are successfully offsetting any maturation in core workloads. A slip below 120% would suggest weakening growth momentum and challenge the long-duration growth thesis. Second, AI revenue contribution and mix will matter - I would like to see steady sequential scaling and evidence that AI workloads are becoming more embedded in production use cases. This may allay some of the fears around Snowflake's positioning in a new AI regime, threat or opportunity. Third, RPO growth and large deal visibility will help gain more confidence around the long-duration growth thesis.

A rating upgrade to buy would require clearer evidence that AI is becoming structurally accretive - either due to sustained reacceleration toward 30%+ growth, improving operating leverage and margins, and an improvement or even stability in NRR. A downgrade back to Sell would likely be triggered if NRR falls below 120%, growth drifts toward the low twenties, or if there are signs that competitive AI pressures are compressing pricing power or gross margins. Q4 may not tilt the balance on either of these factors decisively. However, trend direction will help determine whether this Hold evolves into a Buy or a Sell.

data.title: U.S. Jobs Report Tops Expectations
data.description:
U.S. hiring beats forecasts in January

The January U.S. jobs report, published on Wednesday, surprised to the upside. Payrolls rose by 130,000, a clear step up from December's revised total of 48,000 and well above consensus expectations. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate fell to 4.3%, a historically low level and a sign the labor market remains resilient.

However, the composition of job growth tells a more nuanced story. Gains were concentrated in healthcare and social assistance, with the construction sector also contributing. Meanwhile, higher-paying sectors such as financial services and parts of the information sector saw modest job losses. Those areas are typically associated with stronger wage growth and white-collar demand.

From a U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) perspective, continued job growth and a stable unemployment rate support rates staying on hold for now. If employment is still expanding, it's harder to argue that policy is meaningfully restricting activity. Markets did react, though, with gold extending its recent pullback as the stronger data tempered expectations for near-term rate cuts.

In short, U.S. job growth last month was better than feared. Still, where those gains occurred leaves some uncertainty around the broader outlook.

Markets embrace Japan's growth push

In Japan, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi and the ruling bloc secured a supermajority in the country's Feb. 8 elections. This gave Takeichi's party a mandate to pursue stimulus, tax relief for consumers, investment in strategic industries, and higher defense spending.

In the wake of the election, questions quickly turned to funding. Japan's public debt already exceeds 200% of GDP (gross domestic product), leading investors to wonder if additional spending could lead to more borrowing, higher bond yields and a weaker yen.

The market reaction, however, was more constructive than cautious, with Japanese equities surging to new records. Meanwhile, government bond yields edged slightly higher, while the yen firmed modestly. This combination suggests investors are embracing the potential growth impulse while monitoring fiscal dynamics.

Tech leadership faces closer scrutiny

After leading markets last year, technology shares have lagged as investors rotate away from the most expensive AI-related names. Concerns around valuations, spending plans, and growth assumptions have intensified, particularly following recent capital expenditure announcements.

The largest technology firms are on track to spend more than $650 billion this year, representing a 60% increase from 2025. That scale of investment has sharpened investor focus on returns and execution. Investors are increasingly asking who benefits and who faces disruption. Three key themes stand out:

  1. Large, established software platforms retain structural advantages. Many are deeply embedded within enterprise systems. AI may expand functionality and spending over time rather than replace these platforms outright.
  2. Selectivity is increasing. Investors are gravitating toward businesses that are harder to replicate, including infrastructure and mission-critical back-office tools.
  3. Risks are becoming clearer. If AI lowers barriers to entry or weakens pricing power, profit margins could face pressure. Companies that rely on seat-based pricing models may find it harder to sustain historical revenue growth.

For now, investors are watching practical signposts and asking questions like: Are companies deploying AI at scale? Is AI generating incremental spending? Can revenue grow without simply adding users?

The answers are still emerging. In the meantime, the market is acting like a judge at the Winter Olympics, where strong routines may draw attention, but medals are reserved for those who can actually execute.

data.title: Only 181k Total Jobs Gained For All Of 2025 After Massive Revisions Lower
data.description:
The analysis below covers the Employment picture released on the first Friday of every month. While most of the attention goes to the Headline Report, it can be helpful to look at the details, revisions, and other reports to get a better gauge of what is really going on.

Current Trends

The jobs report showed an increase of 130k jobs in January. This was a beat over expectations but would objectively be considered a weak report. More surprising is the Household Report, which showed a gain of 528k jobs. This is very strong, but it should be taken in context. Last January a similar event happened but to a greater extreme – the Household report showed 2.24M gained vs a 50k loss.

Figure 1: Primary Report vs Household Survey – Monthly

The Household Report has been underperforming the Headline Report for years. That changed in 2025. For the year, the Headline report showed a gain of 181k jobs. To repeat, that is total jobs gained for the year! The Household report showed 2.4M, but excluding the massive gains in January, the reports actually get quite close.

Figure 2: Primary Report vs Household Survey – Annual

The BLS publishes the data behind their Birth/Death assumptions (formation of new businesses). The data is through December of 2025 and December showed an assumed loss of 67k jobs.

Figure 3: Primary Unadjusted Report With Birth-Death Assumptions – Monthly

For the year, the birth-death assumption is a positive 1.15M against negative actuals of -1.08M. This means all positive growth YTD is directly tied to the birth/death assumptions.

Figure 4: Primary Unadjusted Report With Birth-Death Assumptions – Monthly

Digging Into the Headline Report

Unfortunately, despite being highly unreliable, the Headline report is the best data we have for the more recent periods. Furthermore, this is the data the Fed uses to shape its policy. The 130k jobs number was accompanied by a fall in the unemployment rate to 4.3%.

Figure 5: Change by sector

Jobs by Category

When looking at the last 12-month trend, only half of the categories were actually above trend. The big drivers of the gains came from Education and Health, which added 137k total jobs.

Figure 6: Current vs TTM

The table below shows a detailed breakdown of the numbers.

Figure 7: Labor Market Detail

Revisions

This is the biggest story of the jobs report. After all the revisions, the job picture is significantly bleaker than the data originally showed. Not only was every month in 2025 revised down, but 3 of the months had negative growth. Can you imagine how the market would have responded in October to a net job loss of 140k jobs?

Figure 8: Revisions

Over the last twelve months, jobs have been revised down by about 105k per month and revised lower by 98.3k over the last three months! This means that over 1.2M jobs have vanished due to revisions.

Figure 9: Revisions

More Detail in the Household Survey

Another level of detail in the Household report shows full-time vs part-time job holders. The data in the Household report does show that full-time jobs are being added, which is good.

Figure 10: Full Time vs Part Time

Historical Perspective

The chart below shows data going back to 1955.

Figure 11: Historical Labor Market

The labor force participation rate is still well below the highs before the Global Financial Crisis. This month it increased slightly to 62.5%.

Figure 12: Labor Market Distribution

Conclusion

The US economy averaged job gains in 2025 of 15k a month. That is abysmal and pretty much tells you that the economy is in recession. Furthermore, remember the data point above that the gains have come entirely from the formation of new businesses. This means that even the very meager job gains in 2025 exist due to basic BLS assumptions of new jobs based on the formation of new businesses. The US economy is very weak, and the Fed is going to have to step in. This is why gold and silver continue to show strength despite the recent volatility.

data.title: UTF Vs. ASGI: Why A 6% Discount And Rate Cut Cycle Make UTF The Obvious Choice
data.description:
Investment thesis

In this article, I'm going to compare two popular CEF funds that invest in utilities. These are Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund Inc (UTF) and abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund (ASGI). The relevance of the study is related to the fact that they have different investment strategies, though their investment practices are linked to roughly the same assets. Furthermore, based on my forecasts, I expect the upward trend in the technology market to continue in 2026, along with the AI supercycle. The utilities sector benefits from these developments, but also from the fact that the Federal Reserve is likely to continue its cycle of interest rate cuts. Because a fund such as UTF actively uses leverage (29.71%), its borrowing costs are directly affected by interest rates. Therefore, as interest rates fall, leverage becomes cheaper, leading to higher returns on the fund's portfolio. Meanwhile, the ASGI is a new type of CEF that focuses on unleveraged investments in modern infrastructure instead of using leverage.

Fundamental similarities between UTF and ASGI

The main thing UTF and ASGI have in common is their investment concept, with a focus on utilities. Lots of companies in this sector offer services that have inelastic demand. It is a definite advantage for funds investing in such assets, because cash flow generation is more stable (thanks to the more stable profits of utility companies involved in gas, electricity, water supply, and telecommunications). I believe that this investment concept of UTF and ASGI determines their focus on defensive assets, because utilities are the most stable sector of the US economy in the face of a potential crisis or recession. Indeed, during times of crisis, the consumption of utilities by Americans is always necessary to meet basic needs.

Also, the UTF and ASGI have a bunch of other features that make them similar for a lot of income-focused investors. Firstly, their main goal is to diversify globally by adding assets from other regions (not the US) to the portfolio. Secondly, both funds aim to generate consistently high returns, enabling them to pay dividends to shareholders every month, thereby making their shares popular with income-oriented investors. That said, this is where the fundamental similarities between UTF and ASGI end. While they are both CEFs with portfolios focused on utility assets, closer analysis of the funds reveals criteria that distinguish them.

Fundamental differences between UTF and ASGI

First and foremost, what sets these funds apart is their basic structure, and it's important to point out the difference in their portfolios. This is because UTF relies on a hybrid structure, with around 15-20% of the portfolio consisting of preferred shares and corporate bonds. The presence of these assets is important in terms of assessing the potential for generating high returns, enabling the fund to maintain dividends during periods of increased volatility in common shares. Most of the selected holdings are the largest companies in the industry. The UTF managers invest in leaders, dominated by electric power companies (about 30-35%). Important to note is that they represent the most defensive sector with regulated tariffs, whose value is growing due to increased demand for electricity.

ASGI has a completely different portfolio, because this fund strives to make its investments less concentrated, due to which the share of the top 10 assets is 26.7% (as opposed to 28.7% for UTF). The portfolio itself consists of a significantly smaller number of companies (with 55 holdings compared to UTF's 288 holdings). However, managers of the fund aim to have 100% of the portfolio consist of common shares, thus making it more sensitive to market volatility and changes in the capitalization of selected companies. As part of sectoral specialization, the majority of assets are related to transport infrastructure. The proportion of electric power companies is lower than in UTF. Another thing worth noting is that the ASGI portfolio includes assets related to data centers and cloud infrastructure, so it's more dependent on the ongoing AI supercycle. An additional risk factor for the ASGI portfolio is that the invested assets include less well-known companies with greater growth potential but also greater risks due to volatility.

In addition to portfolio composition, another key difference between the funds is their use of leverage. To be more precise, UTF uses leverage as a tool for its operations, while ASGI is a non-leveraged fund. Currently, leverage for UTF is 29.71%, while its cost is 4.83%. By using leverage, the growth phase of the market allows for higher returns. Considering that we are currently in a cycle of falling interest rates, levered costs will decrease, thereby automatically increasing returns. Thus, one could say that UTF has weathered a more difficult period of high interest rates, meaning that its investment appeal will only increase with each Fed rate cut.

The risks associated with changes in P/NAV are also worth discussing in more detail. Historically, the price of UTF has tended to trade around its fair value (NAV). The average P/NAV ratio over a 5-year period had a premium of 0.36%. If we take the last 5 years, UTF shares had a maximum discount of 0.91x and a maximum premium of 1.14x. UTF shares are currently trading at a significant discount, a definite plus for investors, since buying $1 of the fund's assets will cost 94 cents.

As a rule, the ASGI shares have been traded at a discount. Nevertheless, by 2026, the trend has shifted to a fund premium, as I believe, it is due to the significant growth in the price of the shares included in the ASGI portfolio. Over the last 5 years, this fund's biggest discount was 0.82x, with the premium hitting 1.08x. The fund's P/NAV ratio is now at an all-time high. This is a definite disadvantage for investors who are focused exclusively on generating passive income, since even now, it is not as profitable to pay $1.08 for assets worth $1 as it is with UTF. Furthermore, risk of a premium collapse remains, with the possibility of a return to NAV.

Also, the UTF and ASGI differ in terms of their dividend policies, since ASGI has the advantage of higher returns (9.97% vs. 7.00%). Meanwhile, their average annual dividend growth rate over five years is 41.53%. Literally just recently, on February 11, 2026, it was announced that ASGI would increase its payments from $0.21 to $0.22. Forward yield on such a payment is 10.94%.

Comparative table of UTF and ASGI

The table below provides a comparison of the main criteria that distinguish UTF and ASGI. Both qualitative and quantitative indicators are presented here.

Because the key difference between any CEFs is reflected in their total return figures, let's take a look at the difference between them. We'll do this by analyzing the last year, in which ASGI's total return was 39.53% higher. In doing so, these figures should be compared with the passive fund State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU), a benchmark for UTF and ASGI. For the past year, the UTF has outperformed the XLU slightly (by only 1.25%), but the ASGI, in contrast, is significantly ahead (by 39.78%). These are past data, however, and reflect this difference only in 2025, with historical data for 3 and 5 years not showing such an advantage for ASGI. It is also important to consider the difference in P/NAV ratios, with a 6% discount on UTF shares looking more attractive for a current purchase than a 7% premium on ASGI shares. Their combined P/NAV valuation difference is 13%.

Risks of the investment thesis

Given that I have chosen UTF for investment, it is important to analyze the risks associated with investing in this fund.

  • Firstly, I think it's important to consider the high leverage, the cost of which is directly dependent on interest rates. The Fed's decision to pause its rate-cutting cycle means that my expectations for lower leverage costs may not be met, preventing the fund from increasing its returns.
  • Secondly, as the UTF portfolio consists of traditional utility companies, these may be affected by the "green agenda" or frozen electricity tariffs. An important factor contributing to the positive outlook for the fund’s shares is the observed increase in demand for electricity, leading to higher tariffs. Nevertheless, to stabilize prices and alleviate discontent among the population and other businesses, regulatory authorities may suspend tariff increases by limiting prices, even if only for retail consumers, and this would negatively impact the margins of utility companies.
  • Thirdly, coming back to leverage, I should mention that the fund can invest in illiquid securities, which, generally speaking, are not subject to redemption and can make up a large share of investment portfolios. Therefore, at such high leverage, realising illiquid securities leads to financial difficulties that negatively affect the fund's solvency.

Conclusion

Therefore, results of the comparative analysis between UTF and ASGI reveal fundamental differences between them. In terms of cash flow stability, the UTF focuses on traditional utilities and hybrid returns generated, including through leverage. Its portfolio consists of heavier and more reliable companies, rendering it dependent on the trajectory of market capitalization changes in the utilities sector. Shares in ASGI allow investors to bet on more modern infrastructure, which includes AI digital infrastructure, power-saving technologies, and environmental infrastructure. This portfolio consists of smaller companies, the volatility of which is higher. As a result, given the accompanying conditions observed in 2025, its portfolio could grow rapidly. Currently, though, P/NAV shows UTF shares are more attractive, with a surprising 6% discount, whereas historically the fund traded close to par value. Conversely, the ASGI shares have moved from a historical discount to a record premium, rendering them overbought and not favorably priced for a current purchase. As such, the expectation of lower rates with cheaper leverage shapes my bet on UTF, leading me to recommend a "Buy" rating for its shares, with a "Hold" rating for ASGI.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook: Supreme Court Tariff Decision And Key Tests Ahead
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Week in review – Volatile swings and deleveraging continue

Markets have been subject to particular waves of volatility in the past weeks, as years of upward trajectories and the recent acceleration are now questioning extreme valuations. The culprit? Artificial Intelligence.

What had been a boon for stocks is now coming back to bite the investor where it hurts, with investors realizing that AI won't just be an upside-only catalyst.

Productivity gains by AI are now turning into fears of destruction for many firms, industries, and their components – look at tech and software, straight-up bleeding since October.

High investments and capital expenditures that were seen as only positive are now being targeted by profit-takers and short-sellers, as investors question how long it will take to materialize into profits.

This comes amid times when OpenAI, the founder of ChatGPT, is under heavy scrutiny for its fast-paced cash burning, and it gets even more damaging when OpenAI is pretty much the firm that started this entire AI trend to begin with.

In other words, AI won't just allow markets to shoot higher eternally. Some firms and industries will face pain ahead as the global economy prepares for an existential restructuring.

If and when a large number of firms see their activities stolen by revolutionary technology, it could not only lead to massive layoffs and reduced consumption but also trigger cascades of entire industries unable to function correctly, resulting in lower profits and, in turn, an inability to repay their heavy debt levels.

This could quickly turn into a global liquidity and credit crisis – but for now, it's mostly fear: fear of creative destruction, which is for now still lifting global earnings to all-time highs.

To put things into perspective, the Dow Jones still reached a 50,000 all-time high just last week, and Nasdaq is still well below 10% of its record, so for now, it's only a turn in narrative – with stock indexes maintaining a broadly range-bound picture, it's essential to take a step back and not get too afraid too fast.

Markets are all about risk, so too little risk could prevent seizing good opportunities. And in any case, volatility is great, not for investors, but for traders who will enjoy more movement.

The narrative could also change quite suddenly, particularly after Friday's soft CPI print (2.4% vs. 2.5% expected), which could finally wake the soft-landing hypothesis, especially with non farm payrolls beating expectations on Wednesday.

A harsh reality is settling in, and traders will have to keep that in mind when trading: in the times to come, price action will dominate the narratives, so always look at the bigger picture (and check higher-timeframe charts) to stay ahead of what's to come.

Weekly Performance across Asset Classes

Most of the assets that got beaten down last Friday attempted a rebound in this week's action. The best examples of this are, of course, cryptocurrencies and metals, which suffered heavily last week and are up between 3% to 8% on the week.

What also really shifted is the sudden interest in US treasuries, which could launch a wave of repositioning. Bonds had widely been left out of the everything rally in the past three years, bullied by risk assets and elevated inflation, so keep a close eye to see if this changes.

Week Ahead: Global Macro is back

Asia Pacific Markets: RBNZ Rate Decision and Australian Employment

Traders are getting ready for quite a heavy week for APAC trading. Sunday will commence with the Japanese GDP for Q4 where traders will now spot whether the effect of Takaichi's appointment turned into a positive for the Land of the Rising Sun. Looking at how the JPY bounced last week, expect large reactions.

Monday will present the RBA Minutes, pretty important for FX traders, particularly considering that the Australian Central Bank is the only one back to hiking.

The Aussie will also see an important test on Wednesday with its employment figures, essential to track whether the last hike was just a one-off or the beginning of a new cycle.

Tuesday, however, brings the most important test for APAC traders, with the RBNZ rate decision. It will be the first press conference for Anna Breman, the new RBNZ Governor. She will also appear on Thursday, so make sure to listen to how she communicates for further clues.

A reminder that Antipodean currencies (AUD and NZD) have rallied quite severely since the beginning of 2026. They will both face a huge test next week.

Europe and UK Markets: Major data for Great Britain

Despite some key releases for the Eurozone and Germany (surveys and German CPI on Tuesday, key speeches and PPI on Friday), participants will keep focus on UK data. Similar to the US last week, they will face a confluence of high-tier releases.

Starting Tuesday, markets await their employment data, followed by UK inflation data (CPI, PPI and Retail Price Index) before concluding the week with retail sales on Friday.

The releases are all during the overnight session in North America (2:00 A.M. ET), so make sure to keep your risk in check. And of course euro traders will have to stay aware of Lagarde's speech on Sunday (early) morning.

North American Markets: US Core PCE, Supreme Court Decision on Tariffs and Canadian CPI

North American traders will also have some work to do next week. Not mentioning the few speeches (keep an eye on Bowman on Monday), the week really starts on Tuesday with Canadian CPI.

Swap traders are aware that any beat there could significantly add pricing for hikes in Canada for the year to come, particularly after this week's BoC Minutes, where it was noted that the current 2.25% rate is on the "stimulative side".

Turning back to the US, FOMC minutes will be presented on Wednesday, and while they are not expected to be a market mover, they could provide interesting views on inflation, particularly considering how this week's CPI release changed the narrative.

In any case, traders will have to wait for Friday before seeing the release of major data, with a streak of US releases. The weekly closing session will commence with Core PCE (where markets await to see if there is any real improvement in the Fed's favorite inflation tracker).

Promptly after, US services and manufacturing PMIs will be on deck. It has been announced just this Friday morning that the Supreme Court will give the decision regarding Trump's tariffs next Friday. This will be a major market mover.

Even after the latest ease in narrative, keep a close eye on geopolitical developments, particularly those involving the US-Iran talks, as they are likely to continue influencing commodity and broader markets.

Next Week's High Tier Economic Events

Friday 13 Market Wrap

Friday's session brought quite a shift in the market, with the inflation report allowing struggling stocks, cryptos, and metals to rebound. Stocks are however closing unchanged despite the very positive CPI report – this could be a rough sign for bulls.

The best outperformers there are, of course, Ethereum and Bitcoin, which are both coming from very far after last week's tumble. With FX remaining fairly muted, the US dollar will be key to watch next week as traders await a repricing of FOMC cuts in 2026.

If it doesn't reach new lows next week, this sends a sign of relative strength for the greenback, particularly with the upcoming decision on tariffs.

Safe trades, and enjoy your weekend!

data.title: Marvell Technology Q4 2026 Preview: Cooling Growth And Guidance Makes Sense
data.description:
Setting The Stage

Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) is standing at a pivotal position as it prepares to report its Q4 2026 and FY 2026 earnings on 5th March 2026. Being a provider of data infrastructure semiconductors, the company has been riding the AI wave through its custom silicon, electro-optics, and interconnect solutions. However, the last three quarters have shown signs of moderating growth momentum amid broader semiconductor cyclical dynamics.

The stock is trading at about $78.23, down from its 52-week high of $112.5 and losing close to 26% over the last year to underperform the S&P 500. I believe this performance stems from the company’s cooling growth momentum over the last couple of quarters, which I will delve into details shortly.

As we approach the Q4 earnings release, investors are likely to be weighing whether the upcoming report could reignite momentum or near term headwinds such as decelerating growth rates and softer guidance will keep pressure on the shares. With this background, I rate this stock a hold as we wait to see how the upcoming report turns out to be.

A Recap To Recent Performance: Solid But Slowing Momentum

Following what was a strong upward revenue growth momentum building from Q1 2025, where the company had a growth rate of -12.17%, the uptrend lasted until Q1 2026 with a YoY growth rate of 63.26%. The company turned its negative growth rate in Q1 and Q2 of 2025 to a solid double digit growth, which appears to have peaked in Q1 2026, a point at which it started trending downwards to the current 36.83% in Q3 2026.

In my view, this trend is more or less a relative deceleration from peak explosive growth rates rather than an outright stagnation. I tend to believe that explosive growth rates in the north of 50% are unlikely to be sustained in the long run. I think a growth rate in the region of 20%-35% is sustainable and within the mid 20% semiconductor industry long term projected growth rate. In my view, the current double digit revenue growth rate is attractive, and the market could be judging this stock unfairly.

Compared to its peers, I find MRVL to be doing better than them. For example, its trailing YoY revenue growth of 44.95% is better than QUALCOMM Incorporated (QCOM) at 10.25%, and whose quarterly revenue growth has been on a consistent downtrend over the last six quarters from 18.69% in Q4 2024 to 5% in Q1 2026. Compared to Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) with a trailing YoY revenue growth of 23.87%, it follows that MRVL's revenue growth is significantly above its peers and thus superior to theirs. I view the recent quarterly YoY growth as conformity to the normal growth curve, where explosive growth is unsustainable in the long run.

When I look at profitability, I see that MRVL, despite having the lowest operating margin of the three companies, has its margin on an uptrend compared to the peers whose margins seems to be relatively flat, or at the very best, improving at a lower rate than MRVL. In my view, this signals its improvement in operational discipline. As for the net income, its most recent net margin stands at about 91.7%, way above its peers and a significant jump from the region of 10%.

However, it should be noted that this was primarily due to a one-time gain from divestiture. This resulted in a $1.8 billion pre-tax gain from the sale of its automotive Ethernet business to Infineon Technologies, pushing its Q3 2026 net income to about $1.9 billion.

Generally, MRVL has the strongest revenue growth compared to its peers, and the current decline in revenue growth, in my view, is normal, as explosive growth isn’t sustainable. I believe its lower profit margin is due to its lower scale compared to its peers, but having it improve greatly signals it’s catching up. When I talk of scale, MRVL with a trailing total revenue of $7.79 billion, it lags its peers, whose trailing revenue averages more than $50 billion.

This implies that its peers enjoy better economies of scale and hence their better profit margins.

Q4 2026 Expectations & Focus Areas

Recall the Q4 2026 guidance where management guided revenue of about $2.2 billion, implying about 21% YoY growth. This marks a notable drop from the Q3 2026 growth of 36.83%, meaning the management expects its growth rates to keep normalizing, perhaps to the range I had provided earlier consistent with the long term industry growth rate. On the other hand, the consensus revenue estimate stands at $2.21 billion, marking a YoY growth of 21.52%. This is within range with the management’s guidance and in line with my expectation that growth will normalize to about 20%-35%, which is relatively sustainable. This can be further supported by the consensus estimates stretching all the way to 2028 being within my estimated range.

Historically, MRVL has missed its consensus estimates only once over the last 6 consecutive quarters, which sets a good precedent going to Q4. Assuming its last 4 quarterly beat average of 0.58%, I am estimating a revenue target of about $2.22 billion.

On the other hand, its consensus EPS estimate stands at $0.79, marking a YoY growth of 31.36%. This signals the company’s improving profitability. Just like the revenue, Marvell has beaten 6 of its last seven consensus quarterly estimates, making it highly likely to beat its current estimate. Assuming it lasts 4 quarterly average EPS surprises of 1.3%, my EPS projection for Q4 2026 is $0.8. In general, I expect the quarter to be marked by lower revenue growth in line with my growth expectation and industry trends, both revenue and EPS beats, and improved profitability.

As for the key focus areas, I urge investors to keep a close eye on data Centre & AI momentum. This remains to be the company’s growth engine. In Q3 2026, Data Center revenue grew by about 38% YoY to $1.52 billion. Any commentary or updates on accelerating AI CapEx from cloud giants provide a major growth catalyst. Another important area of focus is the 2025 FY guidance. In my view, any revenue guidance in the region of 20%-35% would be within my long term expected growth rate, and this would potentially mark the end of the stock’s volatility since growth could stabilize within that region. Lastly, let’s pay attention to margins. Any signs of further operating margins would support a bullish case, especially at a time when the top line growth is entering the sustainable zone.

Valuation

At about $78.23/share, Marvell is trading at a P/S of 8.76x, significantly lower than AVGO’s 25.16x and higher than QCOM’s 3.37x.

Of the three companies, only AVGO has a positive price return in the last one year of about 40%.

When I take this further and look at the revenue growth rates, MRVL’s trailing revenue growth of 44.95% tops, followed by AVGO at 23.87% and QCOM at 10.25%. Based on this metric, the conclusion is that MRVL is undervalued and warrants a P/S in the north of 25x since its growth is superior to that of AVGO. Let me look at it differently. Over the last three quarters, there is one thing that stands out. The outstanding feature is that, AVGO revenue growth has been on a consistent uptrend, from 20.16% in Q2 2025 to 28.18% in Q4 2025. This has been contrary to MRVL and QCOM, whose growth rates were on a downtrend. For example, MRVL growth has declined from 63.26% in Q1 2026 to 36.83% in Q3 2026, and QCOM’s from 10.35% in Q3 2025 to 5% in Q1 2026.

Given this background, it follows that the market sentiments for both MRVL and QCOM have been negative due to their weakening momentum in revenue growth, and this explains their negative price returns. While I believe having the lowest revenue growth, which is still trending downwards, justifies QCOM’s lowest P/S ratio, I hold a different view for MRVL. Unlike QCOM, Marvell's growth has been in unsustainable region, and I believe its current trajectory signals a stabilizing growth. By it being above AVGO's, I believe that a P/S in the region of 15x-20x is reasonable if the market attaches a premium in an upward revenue growth trajectory.

Risks

Marvell's main risk is customer concentration, with the company deriving a substantial portion of its revenue from a limited number of customers. It is estimated that the top 10 customers account for about 81% of its total sales. In case of slowdowns, delays or reduction in spending by any of these customers, the company’s financials could be impacted materially.

Conclusion

As we approach Q4 2026, this quarterly report and the 2027 guidance will test whether the stock’s recent pullback represents a buying opportunity. From where I sit, the company’s cooling growth rates and management guidance are a reflection of a maturing business whose growth rates must normalize. As such, should the Q4 revenue growth rate and management guidance fall within the 20%-35% with a positive market outlook, I believe that would present a buying opportunity.

data.title: NFIB Small Business Survey: Optimism Remains Above Historical Average
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The NFIB Small Business Optimism Index inched down 0.2 points to 99.3, remaining above the index's historical average for a ninth straight month. This was below the forecast of 99.8 and puts the index at the 50th percentile of the series.

"While GDP is rising, small businesses are still waiting for noticeable economic growth. Despite this, more owners are reporting better business health and anticipating higher sales." - NFIB Chief Economist Bill Dunkelberg

Key Takeaways from January's report:

  • The cost or availability of insurance as the top problem for small business owners was at its highest level since December 2018.
  • The percent of small business owners who reported capital outlays in the last six months was at its highest level since November 2023.
  • The percent of owners who reported paying a higher interest rate on their most recent loan was down from December, suggesting credit markets are turning more favorable for small borrowers.
  • The percent of small business owners reporting labor quality as their top problem fell for a third consecutive month.
  • Actual price increases fell but remain well above the historical average, suggesting continued inflation pressure, while planned prices increased
  • The net percent of owners expecting higher real sales volumes over the next quarter increased.
  • The net percent of owners reporting inventory gains reached its highest level since January 2023.
  • Overall health of business evaluations: Excellent 14%, Good 54%, Fair 27%, Poor 4%

The first chart below tracks the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index since 1986, with a baseline level of 100. Notice the sharp declines in sentiment during the Great Financial Crisis and the COVID-19 pandemic. In contrast, the index showed relative resilience during the 2000-2003 Tech Bubble collapse. Following the Great Recession, small-business sentiment remained weak for an extended period, a pattern that closely resembled the past two years of pandemic-related disruptions and high inflation. Another interesting pattern to the index was the nearly identical jumps following the 2016 and 2024 elections.

The average monthly change in the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index is 1.4 points. To filter out short-term fluctuations, the chart below presents a 3-month moving average alongside the individual monthly values, represented by dots.

NFIB Small Business Survey Components

The NFIB Small Business Optimism Index is composed of 10 components. In January, three components increased and seven declined.

Business Optimism and Consumer Attitudes

The next few charts are overlays of the Business Optimism Index with two of the main measures of consumer attitudes: Conference Board Consumer Confidence and University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index. The Consumer Confidence Index is influenced by employment and labor market conditions from the worker's perspective, whereas the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on employment conditions from the business perspective. (For more information on how these indexes measure up against each other, check out our monthly update, Two Measures of Consumer Attitudes: MCSI vs. CCI).

In our first chart comparing the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index with the Conference Board Consumer Confidence Index, we can see that the consumer measure is the more volatile of the two. Therefore, it is plotted on a separate axis to give a better comparison of the two series from the common baseline of 100.

Next, we compare the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index with the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index. Again, we've plotted each index on a separate axis; however, in this chart, we can see that the business measure is more volatile of the two.

Despite the volatility, though, we can see that these two measures of mood (business and consumer) have been highly correlated, falling and rising together for the most part. A decline in Small Business Sentiment was a long leading indicator for the first two recessions of the century, but clearly not for the COVID-19 recession.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
